text: The surgical team places four robotic ports and an air seal 15 centimeters sub-xiphoid. A Genzyme retractor retracts the liver through the air seal port. Three ports, highlighted in red, are converted to intragastric ports using an 11 millimeter Versastep. The stomach is insufflated endoscopically to 15 millimeters of mercury, and the abdomen is desufflated to align the stomach with the abdominal wall. Under endoscopic visualization, the radially dilating Versastep ports enter the stomach, enhancing traction for the robotic ports placed over them. The stomach remains insufflated to 15 millimeters of mercury for the procedure.

The surgeon uses fenestrated Maryland forceps and hot shears to perform a submucosal resection of a lobulated mass at the gastroesophageal junction. The dissection is carried out circumferentially, with cigarettes used for retraction and blotting. Hot shears are employed for dissection and hemostasis, while robotic arms maintain precision above the gastric muscles. Bipolar or fenestrated Marylins are used to cauterize bleeding when encountered.

The surgeon uses a five-millimeter endocatch bag introduced through the robotic ports to remove the specimen from the submucosa. An endoscope equipped with a snare grabs the end of the endocatch bag, facilitating the transoral removal of the specimen.

The surgeon closes the mucosal defect in two layers using a 3-0 Maxon V-lock suture, tying the ends together. An endoscope is inserted to ensure no narrowing of the gastroesophageal junction. Robotic arms are removed from the intragastric ports under direct endoscopic visualization. The stomach is desufflated, and the abdomen is insufflated. Intragastric ports are closed with interrupted 3-0 silk sutures. A leak test is performed by insufflating air endoscopically through the stomach.
